summary: This comprehensive article defines Serverless architecture as the combination of Backend as a Service (BaaS) and Functions as a Service (FaaS), where organizations reduce server management by leveraging third-party services and ephemeral, event-triggered compute containers. Fowler examines how Serverless shifts responsibility from central server orchestration to choreographed, distributed components, drawing parallels to microservices patterns. The piece methodically weighs benefits—including reduced operational costs, automatic scaling, faster time to market, and environmental efficiency—against significant drawbacks like vendor lock-in, cold start latency, testing complexity, and loss of server-side state. Fowler concludes that while Serverless isn't universally applicable, its reduced feedback loop for delivering new features represents its most compelling advantage for lean development approaches.
key_points: Serverless encompasses two distinct concepts: BaaS (third-party services for auth, databases, etc.) and FaaS (ephemeral, event-triggered function execution), with FaaS being the newer and more architecturally transformative approach
The shift to Serverless moves from orchestration to choreography—instead of a central server managing flow and security, distributed components become architecturally aware and self-managing
FaaS provides automatic, transparent horizontal scaling with pay-per-use economics, but requires externalizing all persistent state since function instances are ephemeral
Vendor lock-in is a legitimate concern but often overstated—the costs of multi-cloud abstraction frequently exceed the benefits of fully exploiting a single vendor's capabilities
Integration testing Serverless applications is significantly harder than traditional systems, and teams must embrace cloud-based testing rather than local simulation
money_quotes: Serverless is, at its most simple, an outsourcing solution.
If your PaaS can efficiently start instances in 20ms that run for half a second, then call it serverless.
Serverless doesn't mean 'No Ops'—though it might mean 'No sysadmin' depending on how far down the Serverless rabbit hole you go.
The sysadmin is still happening—you're just outsourcing it with Serverless.
With a Serverless approach we no longer make such capacity decisions ourselves—we let the Serverless vendor provision just enough compute capacity for our needs in real time.
While the cost benefits are the most easily expressed improvements with Serverless, it's this reduction in lead time that makes me most excited.
Serverless is not the correct approach for every problem, so be wary of anyone who says it will replace all of your existing architectures.
While there are riches—of scaling and saved deployment effort—to be plundered, there also be dragons—of debugging and monitoring—lurking right around the next corner.
key_insight: Serverless architecture's greatest value lies not in cost savings but in dramatically reducing the feedback loop for deploying new application components, enabling continuous experimentation and lean product development.
